fact: The first official Test cricket match was played in 1877 between Australia and England at the Melbourne Cricket Ground.
Sachin Tendulkar is the highest run scorer in international cricket with over 34,000 runs across all formats.
A cricket Test match can last up to five days, making it one of the longest team sport events in the world.
The Ashes is a biennial Test cricket series played between England and Australia, dating back to 1882.
The highest individual Test score is 400 not out, scored by Brian Lara of the West Indies against England in 2004.
A cricket ball weighs between 155.9 and 163 grams and is made of a cork core wound with string and covered in leather.
The ICC Cricket World Cup is held every four years, with the first edition won by West Indies in 1975.
Muttiah Muralitharan holds the record for the most wickets in Test cricket with 800 wickets.
India won the ICC T20 World Cup 2024, defeating South Africa in the final held in Barbados.
The Duckworth-Lewis-Stern method is a mathematical formula used to set revised targets in rain-affected limited-overs matches.
The term 'hat-trick' originated in cricket, referring to a bowler taking three wickets on three consecutive deliveries.
The Indian Premier League (IPL), founded in 2008, is the most valuable cricket league in the world.
A cricket pitch is 22 yards (20.12 metres) long, a unit of measurement historically called a 'chain'.
Rohit Sharma became the first player to score five centuries in a single Cricket World Cup edition in 2019.
The third umpire system, using television replays to adjudicate decisions, was first introduced in Test cricket in 1992.
The first FIFA World Cup was held in Uruguay in 1930, with the host nation winning the inaugural tournament.
Lionel Messi won the FIFA World Cup with Argentina in 2022, completing his collection of every major football honour.
The offside rule in football states that a player is offside if they are closer to the opponent's goal line than both the ball and the second-to-last defender.
Brazil has won the most FIFA World Cup titles, claiming the trophy five times: 1958, 1962, 1970, 1994, and 2002.
Cristiano Ronaldo became the first player to score at five FIFA World Cups, achieving the feat in 2022.
The penalty shootout was introduced at international level at the 1976 UEFA European Championship.
The UEFA Champions League, originally known as the European Cup, was first held in the 1955-56 season.
Real Madrid holds the record for the most UEFA Champions League titles with 15 trophies as of 2024.
The fastest goal in professional football history was scored by Nawaf Al-Abed in approximately 2.4 seconds.
A standard football match consists of two 45-minute halves with additional injury time added at the referee's discretion.
Pelé scored 1,279 goals in 1,363 official and unofficial games, making him one of the most prolific scorers in history.
The VAR (Video Assistant Referee) system was officially adopted by FIFA for the 2018 World Cup in Russia.
The football used in professional matches must have a circumference of 68–70 cm and weigh 410–450 grams.
Vinicius Jr. won the 2024 Ballon d'Or, becoming the first Brazilian to win the award since Ronaldo in 1997.
The first ever international football match was played between Scotland and England in 1872, ending in a 0-0 draw.
India won the Thomas Cup for the first time in 2022, defeating 14-time champions Indonesia in the final.
The shuttlecock can travel over 400 km/h during professional matches, making badminton the fastest racket sport.
Badminton became an Olympic sport at the 1992 Barcelona Games, with four events contested.
The Thomas Cup (men's) and Uber Cup (women's) are the most prestigious team events in badminton, held every two years.
Lin Dan of China is the only player to win the Olympic gold medal, World Championship, and All England Open in the same year (2012).
A standard badminton shuttlecock has 16 feathers, traditionally made from the feathers of the left wing of a goose.
The BWF World Championships have been held since 1977, with China dominating the event over the decades.
PV Sindhu became the first Indian to win two Olympic medals in badminton, claiming silver in 2016 and bronze in 2020.
Badminton is played with a net that is 1.55 metres high at the edges and 1.524 metres high at the centre.
Viktor Axelsen of Denmark won consecutive men's singles gold medals at the 2020 Tokyo and 2024 Paris Olympics.
The sport is named after Badminton House in Gloucestershire, England, where it was popularised by the Duke of Beaufort in the 1870s.
A badminton court measures 13.4 metres in length and 6.1 metres in width for doubles play.
The All England Open Badminton Championships, first held in 1899, is the oldest badminton tournament in the world.
Tai Tzu-ying of Chinese Taipei held the BWF women's singles world number one ranking for a record 207 consecutive weeks.
Modern badminton matches use rally-point scoring up to 21 points per game, a system adopted by the BWF in 2006.
Wimbledon, first held in 1877, is the oldest tennis tournament in the world and the only Grand Slam played on grass.
Novak Djokovic holds the record for the most Grand Slam singles titles with 24, surpassing Serena Williams and Rafael Nadal.
The fastest serve ever recorded was by John Isner at 253 km/h (157.2 mph) at the 2016 Davis Cup.
The four Grand Slam tournaments are the Australian Open, French Open, Wimbledon, and the US Open.
Rafael Nadal won the French Open a record 14 times, earning him the nickname 'King of Clay'.
Tennis was one of the original sports at the modern Olympic Games in 1896, was later dropped, and was reinstated in 1988.
Serena Williams won 23 Grand Slam singles titles, the most by any player in the Open Era.
The longest match in tennis history was the Isner-Mahut match at Wimbledon 2010, lasting 11 hours and 5 minutes over three days.
The term 'love' for zero in tennis is believed to derive from the French word 'l'oeuf', meaning egg, representing a zero.
Carlos Alcaraz won consecutive Wimbledon titles in 2023 and 2024, becoming the youngest back-to-back Wimbledon champion.
A standard tennis court is 23.77 metres long and 8.23 metres wide for singles, extending to 10.97 metres for doubles.
The Davis Cup, founded in 1900, is the premier international team event in men's tennis.
Players must win six games to win a set in tennis, with a tiebreak typically used when the score reaches 6-6.
Jannik Sinner of Italy won the 2024 Australian Open and US Open, ending the year as the world number one.
The yellow tennis ball was officially introduced at Wimbledon in 1986 to improve visibility for television audiences.
Basketball was invented by Canadian-American physical education instructor Dr. James Naismith in December 1891 in Springfield, Massachusetts.
The NBA was founded on June 6, 1946, as the Basketball Association of America before merging with the NBL in 1949.
Michael Jordan won six NBA championships with the Chicago Bulls and is widely regarded as the greatest basketball player of all time.
The three-point line was introduced in the NBA in the 1979-80 season, adding a new strategic dimension to the game.
Wilt Chamberlain scored 100 points in a single NBA game on March 2, 1962, a record that still stands today.
Basketball became an Olympic sport for men in 1936 at the Berlin Games, with women's basketball added in 1976.
LeBron James became the NBA's all-time leading scorer in February 2023, surpassing Kareem Abdul-Jabbar's record of 38,387 points.
The original basketball hoops were actual peach baskets with closed bottoms, requiring manual retrieval after each score.
The shot clock was introduced in the NBA in 1954 to prevent teams from stalling by requiring a shot attempt within 24 seconds.
The Boston Celtics hold the record for the most NBA championships, winning their 18th title in 2024.
A standard NBA basketball court is 28.65 metres long and 15.24 metres wide, with a basket height of 3.05 metres.
Stephen Curry holds the record for the most three-pointers made in NBA history, surpassing 3,700 by the 2024-25 season.
The FIBA Basketball World Cup is held every four years, with the USA winning the most titles.
Nikola Jokić won three NBA MVP awards (2021, 2022, 2024), making him one of the most decorated players of his era.
The first basketball game used a soccer ball; the distinctive brown leather basketball wasn't introduced until 1929.
Field hockey is one of the oldest team sports in the world, with origins tracing back to ancient Egypt, Greece, and Persia.
India is the most successful nation in Olympic field hockey history, winning eight gold medals between 1928 and 1980.
The NHL (National Hockey League) was founded in 1917 and is the premier professional ice hockey league in the world.
Wayne Gretzky, known as 'The Great One', scored 894 goals and 2,857 points in his NHL career, records that remain unbroken.
Field hockey became an Olympic sport for men in 1908 and for women in 1980.
The Stanley Cup, first awarded in 1893, is the oldest professional sports trophy in North America.
A standard field hockey pitch is 91.4 metres long and 55 metres wide, made of water-based or sand-based artificial turf at elite levels.
The hockey puck used in the NHL is made of vulcanized rubber, weighs 170 grams, and is frozen before games to reduce bouncing.
The FIH Hockey World Cup is held every four years, with the Netherlands winning the most titles in both men's and women's events.
India won the FIH Hockey Men's World Cup in 1975, their only World Cup title to date.
Connor McDavid of the Edmonton Oilers is widely considered the best ice hockey player in the world in the 2020s.
The penalty corner, or short corner, is a key set-piece in field hockey awarded for defensive fouls inside the circle.
Germany won the FIH Men's Hockey World Cup 2023, defeating Belgium in the final held in Bhubaneswar and Rourkela, India.
Ice hockey was invented in Canada in the 19th century, with the first indoor game played in Montreal in 1875.
The fastest slap shot in NHL history was recorded by Zdeno Chara at 175.1 km/h during the 2012 NHL All-Star Skills Competition.
The first Formula 1 World Championship season was held in 1950, with Giuseppe Farina of Italy winning the inaugural title.
Michael Schumacher and Lewis Hamilton share the record for the most Formula 1 World Drivers' Championship titles with seven each.
Formula 1 cars can accelerate from 0 to 100 km/h in approximately 2.4 seconds and reach top speeds exceeding 370 km/h.
The Monaco Grand Prix, first held in 1929, is the most prestigious race on the Formula 1 calendar.
Ayrton Senna won three Formula 1 World Championships (1988, 1990, 1991) and is regarded as one of the greatest drivers in history.
A Formula 1 tyre can last as few as 10 laps at circuits like Monaco, where track surface and cornering forces cause rapid degradation.
Max Verstappen won four consecutive Formula 1 World Drivers' Championship titles from 2021 to 2024.
The Drag Reduction System (DRS) was introduced in 2011 to allow drivers to reduce aerodynamic drag and facilitate overtaking.
The constructors' championship, awarded to the team, was introduced in 1958. Ferrari holds the record for the most constructors' titles.
A Formula 1 steering wheel costs approximately $50,000 to $100,000 and contains over 20 buttons, switches, and dials.
The Safety Car was introduced to Formula 1 in 1993 to neutralise races when track hazards require controlled conditions.
The Monza circuit in Italy, known as the 'Temple of Speed', is the fastest circuit on the Formula 1 calendar.
Lando Norris claimed his first Formula 1 race victory at the 2024 Miami Grand Prix, ending a long wait for McLaren.
Formula 1 introduced hybrid power units in 2014, combining a turbocharged V6 internal combustion engine with energy recovery systems.
The pit stop world record for a Formula 1 tyre change stands at 1.80 seconds, set by Red Bull Racing in 2023.
Volleyball was invented by William G. Morgan in 1895 in Holyoke, Massachusetts, originally called 'Mintonette'.
Volleyball became an Olympic sport at the 1964 Tokyo Games, with beach volleyball added at the 1996 Atlanta Games.
A volleyball match is played as a best-of-five sets, with each set won by the first team to reach 25 points (15 in the fifth).
Brazil is the most successful nation in Olympic volleyball history, winning multiple gold medals in both indoor and beach formats.
The libero position was introduced to volleyball in 1998, allowing a defensive specialist to replace any back-row player without counting as a substitution.
The fastest spike in professional volleyball was recorded at approximately 132 km/h by Brazilian player Gustavo 'Giba' Endres.
The FIVB Volleyball World Championship has been held since 1949 for men and 1952 for women.
A standard volleyball court is 18 metres long and 9 metres wide, divided by a net set at 2.43 metres for men and 2.24 metres for women.
Italy won the FIVB Men's Volleyball World Championship in 2022, defeating Poland in the final.
The rally point scoring system was officially adopted in 1999, replacing the side-out scoring system to speed up matches.
Poland's Bartosz Kurek is widely regarded as one of the best outside hitters in men's volleyball in the 2020s.
The Nations League, launched in 2018 by the FIVB, replaced the old World League and World Grand Prix as the premier annual team competition.
A volleyball must have a circumference of 65-67 cm and weigh between 260 and 280 grams.
The USA men's volleyball team won gold at the 2024 Paris Olympics, their second Olympic title.
The jump serve, introduced in the 1980s, revolutionised the game by combining power and spin to make the ball difficult to receive.
Boxing is one of the oldest sports in history, with evidence of organised fighting in ancient Greece dating back to 688 BC.
The Marquess of Queensberry Rules, published in 1867, form the basis of modern boxing, introducing gloves, three-minute rounds, and a ten-second count.
Muhammad Ali, born Cassius Clay, is widely regarded as the greatest boxer of all time, winning the heavyweight title three times.
Boxing has been part of the modern Olympic Games since 1904, with women's boxing added in 2012.
Mike Tyson became the youngest heavyweight champion in history in 1986, at 20 years and 4 months old.
There are four major world boxing organisations: the WBA, WBC, IBF, and WBO, each crowning their own world champions.
Floyd Mayweather Jr. retired with a perfect professional record of 50-0, winning world titles in five weight divisions.
The longest heavyweight title fight in history lasted 110 rounds between Andy Bowen and Jack Burke in 1893, declared a no-contest.
Professional boxing gloves range from 8 oz to 10 oz for competition, while training gloves can weigh up to 16 oz or more.
Oleksandr Usyk became the undisputed heavyweight champion in 2024, defeating Tyson Fury by split decision.
Manny Pacquiao is the only boxer in history to have won world titles in eight different weight divisions.
The term 'round' in boxing originates from early bare-knuckle fights where a round ended when a fighter was knocked down.
Claressa Shields became the first American boxer to win back-to-back Olympic gold medals in 2012 and 2016.
The split-second reaction time of elite boxers is approximately 0.2 seconds, far faster than the average person.
Joe Louis held the world heavyweight championship for a record 11 years and 8 months, with 25 successful title defences.
Chess is believed to have originated in India during the Gupta Empire around the 6th century AD, where it was called Chaturanga.
Garry Kasparov became the youngest undisputed World Chess Champion in 1985 at the age of 22.
The number of possible unique chess games is estimated to exceed 10^120, a figure known as the Shannon Number.
Magnus Carlsen of Norway became World Chess Champion in 2013 and held the title until 2023 when he chose not to defend it.
Deep Blue, a supercomputer developed by IBM, defeated Garry Kasparov in a historic match in 1997, marking the first time a computer beat a reigning world champion.
FIDE, the International Chess Federation, was founded in Paris in 1924 and governs international chess competitions.
The Elo rating system, used to rank chess players, was developed by physicist Arpad Elo and introduced by FIDE in 1970.
The longest chess game theoretically possible is 5,949 moves, based on the 75-move rule introduced in 2014.
Viswanathan Anand became the first Asian player to be recognised as World Chess Champion by FIDE, holding the title from 2007 to 2013.
The FIDE Chess World Cup 2023 was won by Magnus Carlsen, who also won the Candidates Tournament in 2024.
The en passant rule allows a pawn to capture an adjacent enemy pawn that has just advanced two squares, as if it had only moved one square.
Netflix's 'The Queen's Gambit' (2020) sparked a massive global surge in chess popularity, leading to record sign-ups on Chess.com.
Chess has been played in space: cosmonauts on the Soviet Soyuz 9 played a radio game against a ground team in 1970.
D Gukesh became the youngest ever World Chess Champion in December 2024, defeating Ding Liren of China at age 18.
The shortest possible checkmate in chess is 'Fool's Mate', achievable in just two moves by Black against a poorly played White.
